turns.text: Hello Bula, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s quiet right now—the baby is finally napping—so I’m happy to talk. It’s funny, living out here in the woods in Halifax, the silence is usually a gift, but lately, it feels a bit heavy. Being a new mom is already such a singular experience, and being unmarried in a rural spot like this… sometimes I feel like I’m just shouting into the trees.

I went into this pregnancy with this deep, stubborn hope that my birth could be a collaborative, human experience. I did all the reading, all the research, because I knew I had to be my own advocate if I didn't want to just be another "low-risk" box checked off on a hospital chart. I remember one appointment, right at the start—the provider didn't even say hello, just dove straight into a list of routine interventions. I felt my chest tighten. It wasn't just frustration; it was this cold, sinking feeling that my autonomy was disappearing before I’d even had a chance to speak.

I had to stop her and say, "Wait, I need to understand why." It shouldn't have been a battle to be treated like a person, but it felt like one. I’m proud I stood my ground, but God, it was exhausting. I’m just still trying to process how it feels to have navigated the physical birth successfully, only to feel so unseen by the system itself.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

To be honest, I think I was a bit naive. I spent so much energy preparing for the *labor*—researching positions, breathing techniques, and how to advocate for myself—that I almost treated the recovery like an afterthought. In my head, I envisioned a quiet, gentle "lying-in" period. I imagined myself sitting here by the pine trees, breastfeeding in a state of blissful, holistic recovery, maybe doing some light yoga once I felt ready. I really believed that if I approached it with the right mindset, my body would just bounce back to some version of its old self.

The reality hit me like a wall. It wasn't just the physical healing, which was objectively "textbook" and uncomplicated, but the sheer exhaustion of it all. My body felt like it didn't belong to me anymore—it was just this vessel for the baby, constantly being drained. And because I’m here alone in Halifax most of the day, there was no one to hand the baby to when I just needed to lay down or take a long shower without feeling guilty.

The physical pain was manageable, but the *isolation* turned recovery into a psychological marathon. I realized that "recovery" isn't just about tissues and muscles knitting back together; it’s about navigating this profound, lonely shift in your identity when the medical charts are closed and the real work begins.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

You know, everyone talks about the big things—the pain, the stitches, the exhaustion—but no one really warned me about the *loneliness of the physical sensation* itself. I spent months reading about what my body would do, but I wasn't prepared for how disconnected I’d feel from it once the dust settled.

It’s the night sweats, the shifting hormones, and the strange way my body still felt like it was "expecting" to be pregnant even though the baby was sleeping in the other room. There’s this specific, hollow feeling when you're breastfeeding in the middle of the night, staring at the shadows on the wall, and realizing that your body is still being used as a utility. It felt like I’d been through a massive, transformative event, but the medical system just patted me on the back at my six-week check-up like I’d just recovered from a minor cold. 

I was surprised by how much I missed my own skin. That sounds strange, doesn't it? But for weeks, I was just a source of milk, a cradle, or a comforting heartbeat. No one prepared me for that loss of self-ownership. It’s hard to reconcile the "low-risk" clinical label with the reality of feeling like my body has been completely rewritten.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I had this romanticized vision, honestly. I think all the holistic books I read painted breastfeeding as this effortless, primal bond—just me and the baby, perfectly in sync, fueled by nature. I prepared for it like a skill I had to master, thinking that if I just had the right posture and the right information, the flow would be natural. I expected it to be the one thing that felt entirely "mine" and uncomplicated.

The reality was… jarring. The physical reality of it was so much more demanding than any guide could convey. In those first few weeks, it wasn't just about nutrition; it felt like a relentless cycle that kept me tethered to the house. When you’re isolated, as I am, the baby’s need to eat becomes this all-consuming clock you’re trapped under. There were moments—especially at 3:00 a.m.—where I felt completely overwhelmed, not just by the physical pain of it, but by the pressure to be perfect. 

I didn't feel "supported" in the way I needed. I didn't need a medical chart telling me the baby was gaining weight—I needed someone to sit with me, to hear me say it was hard, and to normalize the fact that it was stripping me of my autonomy. It’s a strange, lonely paradox to love the baby so fiercely but feel so resentful of the physical demand.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

Oh, absolutely. The support I was offered felt like a technical checklist—are the latches correct? Are we hitting the weight milestones? Is the supply sufficient? They were so focused on the data, on the "functioning" of the breastfeeding, that they missed the fact that I was drowning.

What I actually needed—what I was starving for—was someone to acknowledge the emotional and social weight of it. I needed to be told that it was okay to feel overwhelmed, or that my identity as an individual was fraying at the edges. I remember sitting in one of those appointments, and the provider was so clinical. I wanted to ask, "Do you see that I’m lonely? Do you see that I’m trying to bridge this gap between who I was and who I am now?" But I didn't say it. I just nodded and answered the questions, because I knew that if I brought up the emotional side, I’d just be another "low-risk" mom complaining about the inevitable.

I needed community support, not just lactation advice. I needed someone to say, "I know you're alone out there in the woods, and that is fundamentally exhausting." Instead, I got sterile, procedural instructions. It made me feel like my struggle was an inconvenience to their schedule, rather than a valid part of the transition into motherhood.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I tricked myself into believing that because I was so prepared—because I’d read all the literature and had a firm grasp on my values—I would be immune to the "blur" people talk about. I expected to feel empowered. I thought the transition would be a hard-won victory of autonomy. I honestly expected that as long as I stayed true to my holistic plan, I’d be steady.

The reality, though? It’s been a constant, quiet oscillation between profound, breathtaking awe and a bone-deep, haunting loneliness. 

There’s this layer of grief I didn't see coming. I’m grieving the version of myself that lived in these woods—the one who could just walk out the door, the one who didn't exist solely for someone else’s survival. And the isolation makes that grief feel ten times bigger. When the sun goes down here, the woods feel like they’re closing in. Some days, I feel like I’m losing my grip on who "Bula" is outside of this kitchen. It’s not just "baby blues"—it’s this feeling of being untethered. I wanted a partnership with my caregivers, but since the baby arrived, I’ve felt like I’m navigating the most turbulent water of my life entirely on my own, just staring at the trees and waiting for a signal that I’m doing this "right."
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before I became a mom, I had this very idealistic, almost intellectualized view of identity. I thought motherhood would just be an *addition* to who I was—a new layer to the person I already built. I imagined I’d just fold the baby into my life, keeping my love for the woods, my interest in wellness, and my independence intact. I truly believed I could just stay "Bula," but with a baby in a carrier.

But the reality has been an absolute, terrifying dissolution. I don’t feel like I’ve added a layer; I feel like the original structure has been gutted. Some days, I look in the mirror and I don’t recognize the person staring back. It’s not just the physical changes; it’s that my entire internal world is dictated by someone else’s hunger, someone else’s sleep, someone else’s moods. 

I’m constantly fighting to keep a tiny, flickering candle of my old self alive. Being unmarried and living out here makes it so much more intense because there’s no one else here to mirror me back to myself, to remind me of who I was before the nursery existed. I’m proud of my baby, more than I can say, but I feel like I’m currently a ghost in my own life. I’m mourning the woman I was, and I’m terrified that she’s never coming back.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the silence that makes it so hard to hold onto my professional identity. Before this, I worked in a role that required me to be sharp, articulate, and constantly engaging with the world. I felt capable, measured, and, frankly, seen. Now, my "work" is entirely internal, repetitive, and hidden away in this rural pocket of Halifax. I didn't expect that my brain would feel so… muted. It’s like I’ve lost the vocabulary for anything outside of infant care, and that scares me.

Socially, the isolation is a wall I didn't see coming. I used to be the person who brought people together, who nurtured my friendships with long conversations and shared experiences. Now, I feel like a social relic. It’s hard to stay connected when your life is governed by a nap schedule and the crushing fatigue of solo parenting. I try to hold onto my love for the woods and my holistic practices—that sense of wellness that grounded me—but even that feels different now. It’s not a lifestyle anymore; it’s a survival mechanism. Everything that used to be a source of joy or identity now feels like a task I’m struggling to keep up with. It feels like I’m constantly dropping pieces of who I was just to keep the baby fed and safe.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected my postnatal care to be a continuation of the partnership I fought so hard for during pregnancy. I truly hoped the follow-up would be a space where I could debrief—not just about my physical stitches, but about the transition. I wanted to sit with my provider and talk about the emotional weight, the shift in my identity, and the isolation of living out here. I wanted them to ask, "How are *you* doing, Bula?" and really wait for the answer.

Instead, the reality was a rapid-fire series of "check-box" appointments. It felt like I was being processed through a machine. They checked my blood pressure, asked if I was healing, and checked the baby’s growth, but the whole experience was hollow. There was no space for the "whole person" I am. I felt like a statistic—the "unmarried, low-risk mother"—and that was it. 

I’d leave the office feeling more invisible than when I walked in. It wasn't that the medical care was wrong, exactly; it was that it was profoundly un-nurturing. They treated the physical recovery like a mechanical issue and completely ignored the emotional turbulence. I was looking for a guide, but I just found another part of the system that didn't know what to do with a woman who had questions about her soul, not just her vitals.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I remember one visit, about four weeks in, when I finally plucked up the courage to mention the isolation. I told the provider, very plainly, "I am struggling with how lonely this is. I feel like I’m disappearing out here in the woods." My voice was shaking—it took so much to admit that—and I was looking for some kind of bridge, maybe a recommendation for a support group or even just a look of understanding.

She didn't even look up from her clipboard. She just gave me a standard, scripted response about "making sure to get fresh air" and "reaching out to friends." It was so dismissive it felt like a physical blow. She didn't ask *why* I felt that way, or if I had any support at all, or even acknowledge that rural isolation is a legitimate hurdle for postpartum mental health. She just checked a box that said my mood was "stable" and moved on to the next patient.

That moment was the breaking point for me. It confirmed my worst fear: that I was just a data point, and my emotional reality wasn't "clinical" enough to warrant real care. I left that office feeling colder than when I arrived. I didn't go back for the next scheduled check-up. Why bother? It felt like I was just performing "the compliant patient" for a system that didn't actually see me.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I’m currently navigating that right now, and honestly, it’s a mess. I thought I had a plan—some remote work, a slow ramp-up, the works. I expected that returning to my professional identity would feel like a breath of fresh air, a way to reclaim the part of "Bula" that isn't just a mother. I thought I’d feel empowered, like I was balancing everything perfectly.

The reality? It feels like I’m constantly failing at two jobs at once. My brain is so foggy from the sleep deprivation that every email takes ten times as long to write. I’m sitting here in my kitchen, the baby is fussing, and I’m trying to sound like a competent, professional adult on a call, but I feel like an imposter. 

Emotionally, it’s been gut-wrenching. There’s this persistent, sharp guilt every time I have to prioritize a project over holding her. I expected to feel relieved to have "me" time, but instead, I just feel guilty for working and then anxious about falling behind because I have to stop to nurse. The system—my workplace—expects me to be the same person I was six months ago, but that woman doesn't exist anymore. She’s gone, replaced by someone who is constantly vibrating with anxiety, trying to bridge the gap between two worlds that refuse to overlap.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

It’s the sheer lack of a physical "on/off" switch that has been the hardest. I thought I could compartmentalize—work for two hours, baby for two hours—but your body doesn't work that way. My milk supply doesn't care about a conference call, and my brain doesn't just switch from "nurturing mother" to "sharp professional" because I sat down at my laptop. 

The physical exhaustion of breastfeeding while trying to engage in high-level thinking is something nobody prepared me for. I’m often sitting here with an ice pack or feeling that physical tug in my chest, trying to maintain a professional composure while my body is essentially still in a state of primal recovery. And the logistics of it? It’s a nightmare. I’m in a rural spot, so there’s no local childcare center I can just pop into, and the isolation means I don't have a village to lean on when a meeting runs over or I need to nap. 

I’m effectively doing two full-time jobs in the same room. I feel like I’m constantly failing both—not being present enough for the baby, and not being sharp enough for my work. It’s a relentless, grinding pressure, and I’m realizing that "flexibility" is just a corporate word for "you’re on your own, good luck."

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

It’s strange, the question assumes there’s a "we." Being never married and doing this on my own, I suppose I expected that maybe the space left by a partner would be filled by a village—that the transition would be communal. I had this picture of friends or family drifting in, helping with the meals, holding the baby while I slept, just being a consistent presence.

In reality, the silence of this house is profound. There is no "we" to negotiate with, which in some ways is a relief—I don't have to argue about how to parent or fight for my share of the rest—but the flip side is that there is absolutely no buffer between me and the exhaustion. Every single decision, every middle-of-the-night cry, every moment of doubt falls entirely on my shoulders. 

I think I expected that I’d be stronger, more capable of doing it all in isolation because of my holistic focus. But the truth is, motherhood isn't meant to be a solo sport. When I’m at my breaking point, there’s no one here to mirror back to me that I’m doing a good job. I’m the only one who sees the hard parts, and I’m the only one who sees the beautiful parts. It’s a very heavy kind of independence.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

Since I am raising my daughter on my own, the "partnership" I’ve had to navigate is really the one between me and the world outside my front door. The biggest surprise has been how much the *absence* of a partner shifts the way the medical and professional systems perceive you. 

When you’re single, there’s this unspoken assumption that you’re a "superhero" who can handle everything without help—or, conversely, that you’re just "at risk." There’s no middle ground where a single mother is just a person who needs support. When I went to those prenatal and postnatal appointments, I felt like the "unmarried" status on my chart was a red flag for some providers, while others just ignored it entirely, as if I didn't have an entire household of responsibilities resting on me alone.

The surprise wasn't that it was hard—I knew it would be—it was that I had to fight just as hard to prove my *competence* as I did to voice my *needs*. It’s a strange, isolating feeling. You want to be seen as a capable mother, but you also desperately want to be allowed to be vulnerable and say, "I am struggling." In my case, there’s no partner to share the burden, and the system isn't designed to bridge that gap. I’m completely on my own.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I went into this whole journey expecting a partnership. I genuinely believed that if I walked in with my research, my autonomy, and a clear vision for a holistic experience, the system would meet me halfway. I expected them to be the experts on my vitals, yes, but also to be human beings who respected that I was the expert on my own body and life. I thought we were building something together—a safe passage into motherhood.

Looking back, the most significant gap wasn't a lack of medical knowledge or skill; it was a total lack of *connection*. They gave me the technical care I needed to get through, but they failed to provide the *relational* care that actually gets a mother through the emotional upheaval of the postpartum period. 

They treated me as an isolated project to be managed, not a person to be supported. I had to fight for every shred of respect and every bit of information. I feel like I survived my birth and the weeks following, but I don't feel like I was *cared* for. It’s devastating to realize that the system is designed to keep the baby safe, but it's entirely indifferent to whether or not the mother is feeling whole, seen, or human. They gave me a healthy baby, but they left me alone to heal the rest of it by myself.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be the way the system is structured around "risk" rather than "humanity." Right now, the system operates on these checklists—vitals, charts, milestones—and if you’re "low-risk," you’re essentially ignored until you raise a red flag. It’s a reactive system, not a proactive one.

I would move us toward a model where every person, regardless of their clinical risk profile, is assigned a dedicated advocate—a navigator, if you will—whose only job is to bridge the gap between the clinical and the personal. Someone who doesn't just look at the blood pressure cuff, but looks at the person sitting in front of them and asks, "How are you *actually* managing your life out there in the woods? Do you need resources? Do you need a place to talk?"

We need a system that recognizes that birth is a transformational life event, not just a medical procedure that concludes with a discharge paper. By the time I left the hospital, I felt like a ghost, left to navigate the most vulnerable months of my life entirely alone. If the system was designed to value the mother’s long-term wellness as much as the baby’s immediate vitals, I wouldn't have felt like I was shouting into a void. I wouldn't have had to fight so hard just to be a person.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the most important thing I can leave you with is how much the "success" of a birth can be a mask for the struggles that come after. People love to tell me, "At least you’re both healthy," and while I am so incredibly grateful for that, it’s often used as a way to silence the rest of the conversation. It implies that if the clinical outcome is good, then the experience itself was a success. 

But I feel like I survived my birth, not that I felt *empowered* by it. There’s a quiet, deep-seated resentment that builds when you realize that your medical team wasn’t interested in your emotional survival, just your biological functions. 

I’m sitting here in Halifax, finally having a moment where the house is quiet, and I’m realizing that the system—those institutions, those appointments—they don’t have to live with the aftermath. They get to close the file on me. But I have to live with the memories of feeling like an outsider in my own labor, of having to fight for my own autonomy, and of the profound loneliness that followed. If you take anything from our talk, I hope it’s that being "low-risk" doesn’t mean a woman doesn’t have high-stakes needs. We aren't just vessels. We're people who need to be held, too.
